feedback.text: I appreciate the effort of the EU to help and improve every day life for the people. The impact is major as could be seen with policies such as USB C adaptation for the iPhone. As such I value the opportunity to weigh in on a matter that is very relevant in current times - namely the online space and the rules set to protect and inform customers. I would like to extend the idea of the already mentioned dark patterns to push customers to decide quickly. I think another problem in the digital space is unclear ownership circumstances. First off it should be clearly and visibly described, whether it is a purchase (with ownership) or renting a license (limited in time or not) or some different system. Especially in the case off an unlimited license as well as digital ownership, in my opinion it should be mandatory to inform customers about estimated license end dates including but not limited to servers being taken offline, duration of license agreements between seller and IP holders and so on. This extends to video games, but also applies to software, movies, music and other digital goods. An end of life plan should be made public informing about rights and functionality after servers are being taken offline or otherwise part of the original functionality is removed or not supported anymore. End of life planning is part of every product life cycle and should be made public to allow consumers to make conscious decisions. Thank you for your consideration.
